CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts the weekend of Nov. 16th lowered snow levels across the region, which brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations across some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hog Pass SNOTEL reported an estimated 12 inches of new snow fell in a 24 hour period ending at 0800 PST on November 18th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF MARION AND LINN COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary followed by a large area of low pressure sitting just off the coast facilitated heavy snow over the Cascades Oct 31 into Nov 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Grass SNOTEL at 4720 ft recorded 13 inches of snow between 1600 PST on 10/30 and 11/01.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF MARION AND LINN COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary arriving on Nov 17th followed by a modified arctic airmass loft and long-fetch northwesterly flow by Nov 18th facilitate heavy snow across the Cascades above 2000-3000ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Grass SNOTEL at 4720 ft recorded 20 inches of snowfall with 12 inches first hit at 0000 Nov 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary arriving on Nov 17th followed by a modified arctic airmass loft and long-fetch northwesterly flow by Nov 18th facilitate heavy snow across the Cascades above 2000-3000ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Hill SNOTEL at 4410ft recorded 19 inches of snowfall with 12 inches first reached 0300 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary arriving on Nov 17th followed by a modified arctic airmass loft and long-fetch northwesterly flow by Nov 18th facilitate heavy snow across the Cascades above 2000-3000ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McKenzie SNOTEL at 4770 ft recorded 16 inches of snowfall with 12 inches first hit at 0100 Nov 18th and the Holland Meadows SNOTEL recorded 20 inches of snowfall with 12 inches first hit at 0400 PST Nov 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure disturbance embedded within southwest flow aloft brought strong winds to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 3CL03 Clatsop Spit near Warrenton: peak gust 69 mph at 11/22 1130 PST, gusts near or above 58 mph from 11/22 1000 to 1430 PST. ODT76 Megler Bridge; peak gust 62 mph 11/22 1127 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level low and associated trough shifted inland supporting a convective post-frontal environment. Enough low level shear and instability was present to support an isolated funnel-cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a report and image of a funnel cloud via a social media post. No reports of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in damage to Highway 6 around 15 minutes west of Banks in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oregon Department of Transportation reported in a KG8 media web article that heavy rain was the likely reason for the sinking and cracking of State Route 6 near the Gales Creek Campground Turnout. The westbound lane was closed November 20th and 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Applegate 3900 Wildfire was discovered on June 20th around 1200 PM PST. The fire started on private land 4 miles south of Ruch, in the Applegate Valley. The cause of the fire is undetermined. As of 530 AM PST on 06/30, it had burned 1,124 acres, was 85% contained, no structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $7M.

The cause of the fire is undetermined. As of 530 AM PST on 06/30, it had burned 1,124 acres, was 85% contained, no structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $7M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought travel headaches for high profile vehicles in Malheur County, especially during an evening cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS at Riddle Mountain measured a gust of 58 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving frontal systems brought several days of heavy rain, which caused flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage of 21.0 ft at 13/1530 PST, and above moderate flood stage of 23.0 ft at 14/0415 PST, crested at moderate flood stage at 23.97 ft at 04/1615 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 16/2145 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from 1/16 into 1/17, another strong low moving southeast from the Gulf of Alaska brought a new episode of heavy rain to Coos County on 1/17 into 1/19, with flooding lingering into 1/20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage of 21.0 ft at 17/1830 PST, and above moderate flood stage of 23.0 ft at 18/1430 PST, crested at moderate flood stage at 23.37 ft at 18/2130 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 20/1130 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front on 01/28 brought heavy rain, which caused flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage of 21.0 ft at 28/1500 PST, and crested at minor flood stage at 21.15 ft at 28/1815 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 29/0700 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving frontal systems brought several days of heavy rain, which caused flooding on the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point rose above minor flood stage of 33.0 ft at 13/2320 PST, and above moderate flood stage of 35.0 ft at 14/0500 PST, crested at moderate flood stage at 35.22 ft at 14/0750 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 14/2040 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving frontal systems brought several days of heavy rain, which caused flooding on the North Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point rose above minor flood stage of 35.0 ft at 14/1040 PST, and crested at minor flood stage at 35.07 ft at 14/1045 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 14/1415 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong, slow moving fronts brought a combination of heavy swell and large wind waves to the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Point Saint George buoy (46027) and the Umpqua Offshore buoy (46229) reported swell sufficient to generate high surf. Surf heights reached 22 to 30 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in advance of a winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind of 46 mph with gusts to 74 mph was recorded at the Summer Lake RAWS at 2338 PST on 1/11. Also, a peak of 42 mph with gusts to 61 mph at 2103 PST at the Summit RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front was nearly stationary over northern Douglas County with warmer air from a Pacific system overriding an existing arctic-influenced air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 0.25 inches of ice accretion was estimated.  Icy roads were reported, with numerous downed tree limbs and trees in northern Douglas County. This included the communities of Curtain, Drain, Scottsburg, Yoncalla, and Elkton. Roads affected included Interstate 5 near the Lane County border and Highway 38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving southeast across Oregon brought high winds to Lake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summer Lake RAWS reported high winds from 1038 to 1938 PST with a peak of 43 mph gusting to 72 mph.  Summit RAWS reported high winds from 1903 to 2203 PST with a peak of 42 mph gusting to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over the eastern Pacific Ocean, and strong high pressure well inland resulted in a tight gradient with south to southwest winds reaching warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flynn Prairie reported peak winds of 31 mph with gusts to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over the eastern Pacific Ocean, and strong high pressure well inland resulted in a tight gradient with south to southwest winds reaching warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over the eastern Pacific Ocean, and strong high pressure well inland resulted in a tight gradient with south to southwest winds reaching warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northerly flow pattern between a ridge centered offshore and a trough over the Rockies resulted in cold, high pressure building over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures included: 32 at Ashland, 30 at Brownsboro, 31 at EW5447 Eagle Point, and 29 at West Wimer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over the eastern Pacific Ocean, and strong high pressure well inland resulted in a tight gradient with south to southwest winds reaching warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buckhorn Springs reported peak winds of 32 mph with gusts to 65 mph, and Acorn Woman Peak reported peak winds of 62 mph with gusts to 87 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the Pacific Northwest on the 8th, ushering in much colder air. Strong and deep onshore flow in this cold air mass led to periods of heavy snow, strong winds for the Cascades, with snow for the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Skywarn Spotters reported an estimated 1 inch of snow at Gearhart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal system moving southeast from the Gulf of Alaska brought widespread heavy snow to elevations above 2000 feet in southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 1 to 9 inches at elevations above 2000 feet. A Snotel report of 9 inches was measured at King Mountain.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough brought about SW winds aloft and thus a strong downsloping wind event across the foothills of the Blue Mountains the night of March 8 and through the overnight hours into March 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cabbage Hill ODOT sensor reported a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front and an upper trough passage resulted in heavy snow showers moving through central Oregon, especially impacting the Bend area and outskirts of Redmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 2.1 miles east southeast of Bend measured 4.1 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0600 PST on Mar. 5th. A Cocorahs observer 6.3 miles north of Bend measured 4.5 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0800 PST on Mar. 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly moving south across the region and the passage of a shortwave in northwest flow aloft led to the development of convective showers developing in the late morning and early afternoon of March 5th. Under these convective showers, at least one landspout was able to develop south of Milton-Freewater in the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one, possibly multiple landspouts were observed by a member of the public west of Highway 11 between Athena and Milton Freewater. Pictures taken show one well defined landspout and another much smaller landspout within it's vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm, occluding frontal system brought snowfall to elevations above 5000 feet in southern Oregon on 02/13 into 02/14. This front hung up over the area then snowfall was enhanced by another strong system from 02/14 into 02/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 10 to 26 inches at elevations above 5000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone ranged from 1.60 inches at Diamond Lake to 1.80 inches at Summit Lake, and 2.00 inches at Annie Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and strong cold front brought a round of heavy snow to the Pacific Northwest mountains February 25th through the night of February 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Milk Shakes SNOTEL reported 12 inches of new snow between 9PM February 25th and 11AM February 26th. High Ridge SNOTEL reported 10 inches of new snow between 2AM and 7AM February 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low over British Columbia pushed southward into the Pacific Northwest. This brought low snow levels to portions of southwest Washington and northwest Oregon, and the Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 2.5 inches snow from February 14th to 15th.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hogg Pass SNOTEL reported 14 inches of new snow between 4PM February 25th and 2PM February 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding front moving northeast to the northern California coast then into southern Oregon brought an advisory level snowfall to Lake County, but also a period of high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 6 inches was accompanied by gusty southwest winds. High winds at Summer Lake RAWS on 02/05 from 0638 to 0938 PST peaked at 33 mph with gusts to 62 mph. Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.09 inches at Lakeview to 0.17 inches at Summer Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter systems starting with a strong arctic front followed by an upper level trough brought heavy snow accumulations and very cold temperatures across the mountains and many lower elevation locations across northeast Oregon and southeast Washington between Jan. 10th and the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported via Facebook that 7 inches of new snow had fallen at 1414 PST on Jan. 12th. The Fire Chief in Mitchell had reported 9 inches of new snow had fallen, with heavy snow ending around 2000 PST on Jan. 13th. A public report from Mount Vernon indicated 11 inches of storm total snowfall had fallen at 2300 PST on Jan. 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska brought heavy snow to southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 4 to 13 inches. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.29 inches at Lakeview, 0.45 inches at Christmas Valley, 0.39 inches at Fort Rock, and 0.70 inches at Summer Rim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that moved southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska and brought lowering snow levels and gusty winds with heavy snow to southern Oregon continued east of the Cascades early on 03/01, and was followed by a strong trough associated with the axis of an upper low. This slow moving secondary low brought additional heavy snow, lingering longest in Josephine County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 1 to 12 inches at elevations above 2500 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.70 inches at Devil's Knob,  0.30 inches at Burnt Ridge, 0.33 inches at Signal Tree, and  0.94 inches at Lane Mountain.

This slow moving secondary low brought additional heavy snow, lingering longest in Josephine County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 5 to 26 inches at elevations above 2500 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.61 inches at Calvert Peak, 1.75 inches at East Cave Junction, and 3.62 inches at Sexton Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts, especially around Springfield which received well over an inch of ice accumulation. Strong east winds in the Portland metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries and fatalities during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and electrocution from fallen power lines.  The State of Oregon estimated economic loss to businesses of $165 million due to winter weather for the month of January. While this was for the entire state, most of the impacts were in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cascade valleys had varying amounts of snow and ice  - with reported ice accumulations around 1 inch, reaching crippling levels for portions of Lane county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts, especially around Springfield which received well over an inch of ice accumulation. Strong east winds in the Portland metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries and fatalities during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and electrocution from fallen power lines.  The State of Oregon estimated economic loss to businesses of $165 million due to winter weather for the month of January. While this was for the entire state, most of the impacts were in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard-like conditions were observed at the height of the storm with roughly 1 to 4 inches of a snow/sleet across the Portland and Vancouver metro area and widespread east wind gusts of 35-55 mph. This led to hundreds of downed trees and limbs, resulting in widespread and extended power outages. Multnomah County, the City of Portland, City of Lake Oswego declared a State of Emergency. Portland Parks and Recreation reported 380 downed trees or large branches, The City of Lake Oswego reported more than 100 downed trees. These reports along with trees downed on private property, at least 500 trees were downed in the Portland Metro area.  Downed trees and power lines resulted in power outages. Portland General Electric had a peak count of 165,000 customers without power. Portland Bureau of Transportation had 29 road closures due to downed powerlines and trees within the city of Portland, TriMet cancelled all public transportation. Additional roads were closed across Washington and Multnomah Counties including parts of Highway 30 and US 26. At least 10 homes and 25 cars were damaged from downed trees. Damage estimates are from counties of Multnomah & Clackamas which have boundaries outside the NWS zone. There was no cost estimate from Washington county for this report.||There were 7 direct fatalities due to either suspected hypothermia, falling objects. Two indirect fatalities attributed to a generator used to warm an apartment caught fire. Another indirect fatality occurred when a tree fell on a RV, starting a fire killing one of two occupants. Three direct injuries occurred when a tree fell on a tent injuring the occupants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts, especially around Springfield which received well over an inch of ice accumulation. Strong east winds in the Portland metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries and fatalities during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and electrocution from fallen power lines.  The State of Oregon estimated economic loss to businesses of $165 million due to winter weather for the month of January. While this was for the entire state, most of the impacts were in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and sleet, along with ice accumulations of up to one inch led to power outages, transportation disruptions across the Eugene, Springfield, Cottage Grove and Corvallis areas. Interstate 5 was closed at MP 209 (Linn County) due to truck crashes. Power outages continued through at least January 22nd where 92 percent of EWEB customers that lost power were restored at that time. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts, especially around Springfield which received well over an inch of ice accumulation. Strong east winds in the Portland metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries and fatalities during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and electrocution from fallen power lines.  The State of Oregon estimated economic loss to businesses of $165 million due to winter weather for the month of January. While this was for the entire state, most of the impacts were in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two feet of snow through the period. Sharp wind shift early the 13th resulted in bitter cold wind chills as low as -30��F, requiring ski areas to shut down.  During the period January 8-13, the Oregon Cascades above 2000' generally received 2-5 feet of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts, especially around Springfield which received well over an inch of ice accumulation. Strong east winds in the Portland metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries and fatalities during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and electrocution from fallen power lines.  The State of Oregon estimated economic loss to businesses of $165 million due to winter weather for the month of January. While this was for the entire state, most of the impacts were in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions severely impacted travel on I-84 through the Columbia River Gorge. East winds gusted as high as 75 mph at the west end of the Columbia River Gorge. The Sternwheeler Columbia Gorge river cruise boat���s moorings broke due to strong winds in the Cascade Locks Marina  and crashed into a footbridge. The winds resulted in downed trees/powerlines and power outages. The City of Cascade Locks had city-wide power outage (1500 homes).  City of Corbett���s potable water infrastructure was disrupted due to freezing and electrical outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About one-half of an inch of ice accumulation was inferred from the Portland ASOS, and across the Greater Portland Metro Area from the 16th to 17th. This resulted in travel disruptions, slip and fall injuries, and power outages.  In addition to the ice, persistent freezing temperatures over several days led to frozen/burst water pipes and resulting damage to some homes and businesses.  There were 4 known fatalities, 3 of which occurred when a tree branch fell, due to ice accumulation, on a power line which then fell on the car with 4 occupants. Three were electrocuted when they attempted to exit the vehicle. The fourth occupant was an 9 month old who survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a persistent area of low pressure still centered offshore of southern Oregon and far northern California, mid-level moisture moving northward with an unstable air mass lead to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the Rogue Valley cities of Phoenix, and Central Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2 dozen pine trees of 1 to 2 ft. diameter snapped or fell, as well as a 3.5 to 4 ft. diameter tree that fell and demolished a fence. A tree fell on a power line in Central Point, which destroyed a mobile home and caused a 15 acre wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure brought excessive heat to the coastal/Coquille Valley portion of the south central Oregon coast from July 4th through July 9th. The excessive heat extended all the way to the coast during the peak of the heat wave from July 5th through July 6th. Low temperatures were in the lower 50s to lower 60s, and high temperatures mainly in the mid 80s to mid 90s, except 100 to 107 degrees in the Coquille Valley on July 5th and 6th. Record high temperatures were reached at the Southwest Oregon Regional Airport in North Bend of 84 degrees on July 5th and 86 degrees on July 6th. The Oregon State Medical Examiner's Office reported a potentially heat related death of a 33 year old male on July 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to Eastern Curry County and Josephine County for 6 consecutive days from July 4th through July 9th, and again on July 13th. Advisory level conditions occurred on July 10th through 12th. During the excessive heat, low temperatures were generally in the lower 50s to lower 60s, and high temperatures from 100 to 110 degrees. But, peak readings were recorded on July 6th of 115 degrees at the Agness RAWS, 112 at the Merlin RAWS, and 114 degrees at the Parkside School in Grants Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.

Advisory conditions occurred from July 10th through July 12th. During the excessive heat, low temperatures were generally in the lower 50s to lower 60s, and high temperatures from 100 to 110 degrees. But, peak reading were recorded on July 6th of 112 degrees at the Rogue Valley International Airport, and 113 degrees in the cities of Rogue River and Central Point. Record high temperatures at Rogue Valley International Airport in Medford of 105 degrees on July 4th, 109 degrees on July 5th,  112 degrees on July 6th, 108 degrees on July 7th, 108 degrees on July 8th, and 107 degrees on July 9th. The Oregon State Medical Examiner's Office reported potentially heat related deaths of a 54 year old male on July 6th, and a 55 year old male on July 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered offshore of southern Oregon and far northern California sent mid-level moisture northward and created an unstable air mass, leading to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the Rogue Valley cities of Central Point, and Eagle Point. About 7000 customers in the Rogue Valley lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 2 inch diameter tree branches breaking off, a large tree split in half at a Central Point city park and damaged picnic tables. Also, a home on willow springs road was destroyed when a Douglas Fir of approximately 2 feet in diameter landed on the roof.  This caused a bowing and break of the roof, and upheaval of the foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered offshore of southern Oregon and far northern California sent mid-level moisture northward and created an unstable air mass, leading to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the Rogue Valley cities of Central Point, and Eagle Point. About 7000 customers in the Rogue Valley lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18 inch diameter tree was uprooted and took down power lines on Nick Young Rd. in Eagle Point, trapping a person in a car until they could be rescued.  About 20 trees were reported down, including one that demolished a fence and another couple that damaged roofs on houses. Also, 7 roads were closed and power outages occurred that lasted 6 to 9 hours. An awning was ripped from its foundation and landed on a roof.  1 inch in diameter healthy tree branches were ripped off and tents damaged and blown away at a fire camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure aloft setup over the West Coast, centered over portions of California. This system was nearly stationary which brought a prolonged stretch of hot temperatures to northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Temperatures ranged from 95 to 105 degrees during this time.  There were 12 suspected heat related fatalities with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hottest period was from the 5th to 9th where high temperatures ranged from 99 to 104 in Portland. In addition to the hot daytime temperatures, overnight low temperatures were well into the 60s offering little cooling for those without air conditioning. This time of year the normal high is around 80 degrees and the normal low is around 57 degrees. The State Medical Examiners reported 1 suspected heat related fatalities in this zone.

Record high temperatures were reported at Klamath Falls International Airport of 102 degrees on July 6th, and 101 degrees on July 7th. Records were tied at 98 degrees on July 9th, and 100 degrees on July 13th. The Oregon State Medical Examiner's Office reported a potentially heat related death of a 27 year old male on July 9th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF MARION AND LINN COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary followed by a large area of low pressure sitting just off the coast facilitated heavy snow over the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Grass SNOTEL at 4720 ft recorded 13 inches of snow between 1600 PST on 10/30 and 1700 PST on 10/31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow and afternoon instability lead to some severe thunderstorm formation over the southern Cascades. ||The threat of hail from these thunderstorms disrupted wildfire response across the area. Some of the wildfires grounded helicopter and aviation response completely, crews were pulled off of the line, some teams only worked at night, and some not at all. This delay in wildfire response is estimated to cost wildfire management agencies $750,000 across the region that experienced severe thunderstorms.  ||In addition, power outages were reported across the region Pacific Power reporting 6,290 customers without power and Portland General Electric reporting 3,700 customers without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from start to end of 1 inch Hail including a report from the Coffee Pot Wildfire and photos from the Public on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow and afternoon instability lead to some severe thunderstorm formation over the southern Cascades. ||The threat of hail from these thunderstorms disrupted wildfire response across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary passed through the region on October 30th followed by a quick transition to an unstable post-frontal airmass with cold air advecting into the region aloft in the afternoon hours. The convection conditions and weak shear produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports, from various public sources, reported observing at least a funnel cloud in-between Corvallis,OR and Philomath,OR. Radar did show possible rotation around this time. Location point used is a best estimate based on information from the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable post-frontal airmass facilitated numerous showers and thunderstorms along the coast on the morning of October 31st. Shear in the lower levels of the atmosphere combined with a stronger convective cell to produce a weak and short lived Tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Oregon State University Ship Operations Facility in South Beach, Oregon, security camera footage showed a distinct circulation move northeastward across a lot before moving over the bay and impacting pilings and a dock in the water. About 20 square feet of concrete shingles were torn off the main building. Various items weighing up to 1100 pounds in the parking lot were flipped upside down or pushed aside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lexington ASOS, along with co-op stations G0235 in Condon and D5444 in Heppner, reported subfreezing temperatures between 5am and 8am the morning of the 25th.

The river dropped below moderate flood stage at 31/1500 PST, and remained above minor flood stage at the end of the month.

The collision resulted in one fatality. Estimated snowfall in the previous 12 hours of 2 to 4 inches would be representative of Advisory conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective and unstable post-frontal environment saw shower and isolated thunderstorm activity enhance on the evening of Nov 11th as the axis of the upper-trough shifted overhead. Enough instability and low-level shear was present to produce an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF-0 tornado with estimated winds of 85 mph. The tornado briefly touched down in Rockaway Beach, OR. It occurred around 9:24 PM 11/11/24 and lasted one to two minutes or so according to the spotter that reported it. KRTX radar imagery depicted a fairly strong velocity couplet over Rockaway Beach. This likely formed as a waterspout then made landfall, though the path was not continuous. There were multiple reports of damage to fences, roof shingles, and a window and dirt/debris being tossed around. Upon further investigation, more security camera video footage was received from the Neah-Kah-Nie School district showing distinct rotation of a tornado moving through a parking lot where several items were thrown or moved including portions of a baseball dug-out, porta-potty, and several dumpsters. A garage on the premises had its roof blown off and the garage doors blown in. One minor injury was reported. The maximum width of the the tornado is estimated at 150 yards and an estimated path length of 0.25 miles based on damage reports.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Point Saint George buoy (46027) and the Umpqua Offshore buoy (46229) reported swell sufficient to generate high surf. Surf heights reached 25 to 30 feet. Waves were highest on south facing beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with deep low pressure moving toward Vancouver Island brought high winds to southern Oregon and northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded with peak south winds of 40 mph gusting to 80 mph at Flynn Prairie RAWS. Also, peak south winds of 44 mph gusting to 74 mph at Gold Beach Municipal Airport, a gust of 61 mph at Milepost 327.5 on US101 in Gold Beach, and a gust of 64 mph at Red Mound RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with deep low pressure moving toward Vancouver Island brought high winds to southern Oregon and northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported at Long Prairie RAWS with peak speeds 38 mph gusting to 82 mph. Also, Beaver Hill with a peak of 37 mph gusting to 68 mph, and Port Orford at 59 mph gusting to 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with deep low pressure moving toward Vancouver Island brought high winds to southern Oregon and northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South winds reached High Wind criteria over much of Lake County. Peak reports included: Summit RAWS at 37 mph gusting to 65 mph, Summer Lake RAWS at 39 mph gusting to 73 mph, Lakeview BLM Fuels RAWS gusting to 66 mph, Highway US20 at Bucks Creek at 37 mph gusting to 59 mph, Highway OR31 in Paisley at 33 mph gusting to 64 mph, and Highway 395 in Valley Falls at 44 mph gusting to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding front moved across southern Oregon on 11/17, followed by a post-frontal trough with lowering snow levels on 11/18. This brought a winter storm to the southern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 4 to 10 inches at elevations between 2500 and 3000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.70 inches at Toketee Airstrip, 0.55 inches at Toketee Lake, 1.01 inches at Buckeye, and 1.25 inches at Grandad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding front moved across southern Oregon on 11/17, followed by a post-frontal trough with lowering snow levels on 11/18. This brought a winter storm to the southern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 8 to 16 inches. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.40 inches at Annie Springs, 2.40 inches at Summit Lake, and 1.20 inches at Diamond Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding front moved across southern Oregon on 11/17, followed by a post-frontal trough with lowering snow levels on 11/18. This brought a winter storm to the southern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 2 to 16 inches, with the highest amounts in the southern Oregon Cascades near Highway 140 and Lake of the Woods northward. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 2.40 inches at Fourmile Lake, 2.00 inches at Fish Lake, 2.00 inches at Sevenmile Marsh, and 1.60 inches at Cold Springs Camp.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong band of SW flow aloft associated with a deep offshore low allowed for very strong wind gusts to occur across the foothills of the Blue Mountains during the overnight period of November 19-20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple ODOT sensors and Co-Op observers along the base of the Blues recorded damaging wind gusts from the afternoon of November 19 to the evening of November 20, peaking at 85 mph at AS881 in Mission. I-84 was also closed for a period of time due to an accident caused by the strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered offshore of southern Oregon sent mid-level moisture northward and created an unstable air mass, leading to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the city of Lakeview in Lake County, Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm along North 4th St, Highway 140, in Lakeview, Oregon generated strong downdraft straight line winds.  Power lines were tipped over and tree limbs were down, resulting in 710 customers that lost power.  The roof blew off the Pacific Power office building, into the road.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with deep low pressure moving toward Vancouver Island brought heavy snow and strong winds to much of southern Oregon and northern California with snow levels around 3500 to 4000 feet. The front was slow-moving with snow levels gradually rising during 11/20/2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 5 to 12 inches. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.10 inches at Chemult, 1.00 inches at Silver Creek, 0.80 inches at Crazyman Flat, and 0.40 inches at Quartz Mountain. Also, south winds were strong with gusts of 25 to 40 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 6 to 20 inches. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 2.00 inches at Annie Springs, 1.00 inches at Summit Lake, 1.00 inches at Diamond Lake, and 1.29 inches at Clearwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with deep low pressure moving toward Vancouver Island brought heavy snow and strong winds to much of southern Oregon and northern California with snow levels around 3500 to 4000 feet. The front was slow-moving with snow levels gradually rising during 11/20/2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 4 to 18 inches, with the highest amounts at elevations above 4000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 3.00 inches at Biegelow Camp,  0.90 inches at Fourmile Lake, 1.60 inches at Sevenmile Marsh, and 1.10 inches at Howard Prairie. Also, south winds were very strong with a peak gust of 89 mph recorded at Acorn Woman Peak, and 62 mph at Parker Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air flowing in from behind a departing trough made for freezing temperatures overnight into the morning of September 30 across the Wallowa Valley. the Grande Ronde Valley, and central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redmond airport ASOS reported freezing and subfreezing temperatures for over a 3 hour period around 4 am and 7 am. Similar readings can be found at the Bend, Prineville, and Madras stations as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air flowing in from behind a departing trough made for freezing temperatures overnight into the morning of September 30 across the Wallowa Valley. the Grande Ronde Valley, and central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Joseph, OR AWOS reported freezing and sub-freezing temperatures around 1:30 am and 7:30 am. Similar readings were found at co-op stations CW1102 in Wallowa and EW8320 in Lostine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge over the western states with a thermal trough extending from northern California to near the southern Oregon coast brought a few days of hot temperatures, with extreme heat during the hottest day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to Central Douglas County during the hottest day of the episode on September 5th. Low temperatures were generally in the mid 50s to lower 60s, and high temperatures in the upper 90s to 105 degrees. A record high temperature was set at Roseburg Regional Airport of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge gradually built into the region from the eastern Pacific Ocean with weak winds and clear skies. This resulted in freezing temperatures at the Douglas County, and Coos County coastal areas, and into the Coquille Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures were in the upper 20s to lower 30s, including readings of 28 at the Southwest Oregon Regional Airport in North Bend, 28 at the USCRN Site near Coos Bay 8SW, 28 at FW9247 Lakeside, and 28 at FW9441 Myrtle Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air flowing in from behind a departing trough made for freezing temperatures overnight into the morning of September 30 across the Wallowa Valley. the Grande Ronde Valley, and central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Grande ASOS reported freezing and subfreezing temperatures between 5 am and 7:30 am. Co-op station D6879 in Union, OR read similar values as well.
